proposal.reasoning: This is the first iteration in the exploration phase with no prior data. To maximize information gain, I propose 8 diverse parameter sets covering distinct physical regimes: (1) Baseline middle values for reference, (2) Strong cohesion (high epsilon magnitude, small rmin) to test density/HVap upper bounds, (3) Weak cohesion (low epsilon, large rmin) to test lower bounds, (4) Maximum dipole (long dummy bonds, high charges) to target the 3.72 D dipole moment, (5) Minimum dipole (short bonds, low charges) to test dipole sensitivity, (6) Stiff angles (high ktheta) to test rigidity effects on surface tension, (7) Flexible angles (low ktheta) to test conformational entropy effects, and (8) A balanced 'best guess' combining moderately strong cohesion with enhanced dipole. This Latin Hypercube-style sampling will identify which parameter classes most strongly influence target properties (density, HVap, surface tension, dipole) while respecting all physical constraints (charge balance, dummy bead positioning within sigma/2, kb > 100).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Thermodynamic properties (density, heat of vaporization, surface tension) are primarily governed by cohesive energy parameters. Increasing the magnitude of epsilon (making it more negative) strengthens London dispersion interactions, leading to higher density and heat of vaporization. Decreasing rmin enables tighter molecular packing, further increasing density. Electronic properties (dipole moment) depend on charge separation distance (controlled by CNI_DUP_bl and MOC_DUN_bl) and charge magnitude (DUP_charge/DUN_charge). By systematically varying these orthogonal parameter groups across 8 forks, we can deconvolute their relative contributions to the target properties.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Forks 2-3 will establish the sensitivity of thermodynamic properties to cohesion strength. Forks 4-5 will bracket the dipole moment target (3.72 D). Forks 6-7 will reveal how molecular rigidity affects surface tension and packing. Fork 8 tests a synergistic combination. This parallel evaluation will identify high-impact parameters for subsequent refinement, avoiding crash regions while exploring the physically valid parameter space.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by simulating all 8 parameter sets in parallel and comparing calculated density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment against experimental targets (298K: ρ=0.9361, ΔHvap=10.951, γ=32.43, μ=3.72; 313K: ρ=0.9241, ΔHvap=10.743, γ=31.56, μ=3.72).
